children.name: Hypothymis
children.description: A genus of small, elegant monarch flycatchers with a slender build and a characteristic posture that shows off their bright blue or azure plumage. Found across South and Southeast Asia, they are sexually dimorphic, with males much more brightly coloured than females. They hawk for insects in the forest understorey and are often members of mixed-species flocks.

children.name: Hypothymis azurea
children.description: A small, graceful monarch with the male a brilliant azure blue with a black nape band and a black bill, found across South and Southeast Asia. It is an active, acrobatic insectivore that gleans and sallies for prey in the forest understorey. Its clear, whistled call carries through the forests where it resides.

children.name: Terpsiphone
children.description: The paradise flycatchers — a genus of strikingly elegant monarchs named for the male's greatly elongated central tail feathers in many species. They are found across Africa, Asia, and the Indian Ocean islands. Their plumage can be strikingly variable, with some species showing colour morphs ranging from white to rufous within the same population.

children.name: Terpsiphone atrocaudata
children.description: A graceful monarch with elongated central tail feathers, the male a glossy black with a white belly and a blue eye-ring, breeding in Japan, Korea, and Taiwan and wintering in Southeast Asia. It catches insects in mid-air with elegant sallies from exposed perches in the forest canopy. Its simple, whistled song is a characteristic sound of its temperate forest breeding grounds.

children.name: Terpsiphone viridis
children.description: A stunning flycatcher with the male showing two colour morphs — rufous or white — both with a glossy blue-black head and long flowing tail streamers, widespread across sub-Saharan Africa. It is an active, vocal bird of woodlands and gardens, often seen in noisy family parties after breeding. Its harsh, rasping call is an unexpected contrast to its elegant appearance.

children.name: Grallina
children.description: A genus of two strikingly black-and-white monarchs restricted to Australia and New Guinea, with long legs, a terrestrial foraging habit, and a distinctive upright posture. Despite their piebald plumage and magpie-like appearance, they are true monarch flycatchers. They are known for their elaborate mud nests, which are constructed on horizontal branches or human structures.

children.name: Grallina cyanoleuca
children.description: A familiar black-and-white bird with a thin white bill and a melodious, piping call, found across Australia and southern New Guinea. Despite its common name, it is not a magpie nor a lark but a monarch flycatcher that forages on the ground for insects. It builds a distinctive mud bowl nest lined with grass and feathers, often placed on a horizontal branch.

children.name: Monarcha
children.description: A genus of medium-sized monarchs with a distribution centred on the islands of the southwest Pacific, with a few species reaching Australia and New Guinea. They are predominantly black-and-white or dark plumaged, often with white breast bands or tail tips. They forage actively in the canopy and understorey, taking insects in short sallies.

children.name: Monarcha cinerascens
children.description: A medium-sized, grey-brown monarch with a white throat and breast, a dark eye-stripe, and white edges to the tail, found on islands from Sulawesi to the Solomons. It is an adaptable monarch that inhabits forests, mangroves, and secondary growth across its extensive island range. It forages actively in the canopy, sallying for insects.

children.name: Myiagra
children.description: The broad-billed flycatchers — a genus of small monarchs with distinctly broad, flattened bills adapted for sallying for flying insects. They are found from Southeast Asia through Australasia to the Pacific islands. They are restless foragers, constantly on the move and flicking their wings and tails, with many species showing glossy blue-black or metallic upperparts in the males.

children.name: Myiagra alecto
children.description: A striking monarch with the male a deep iridescent blue-black and the female a rich rufous-orange above with a white throat and breast, found in northern Australia and New Guinea. It inhabits dense vegetation along waterways, mangroves, and rainforest edges. It sallies from low perches to catch insects in flight.

children.name: Myiagra ruficollis
children.description: A small monarch with a noticeably broad, flat bill, slate-blue upperparts, and a rufous-orange throat and breast, found in coastal northern Australia, New Guinea, and the Lesser Sundas. It is a specialist of mangroves and coastal forests, where it hunts insects in short, swift sallies from exposed perches. Its broad bill is an adaptation for catching flying insects in dense coastal vegetation.
